fingerprint: CERES measurement framework is globe-dependent. The +0.34 W/m²/decade figure the dome adopts is computed by integrating satellite radiances over a spherical Earth using globe-geometry Angular Distribution Models. Furthermore, the hemisphere definition itself encodes globe geometry: on the dome's disc (AE projection), NH and SH have area ratio 1:3, not 1:1 as on the globe — any per-unit-area hemispheric comparison gives different numbers on a disc.
globe_value: CERES TOA flux: hemispheric averages computed over equal-area hemispheres (2πR² each ≈ 2.55 × 10⁸ km²) using ADMs calibrated for a spherical Earth
dome_value: Dome claims 'NH heat excess confirms dome asymmetry' — adopting the globe-framework measurement wholesale. On the dome's disc, NH area = π(10,008)² ≈ 3.15 × 10⁸ km², SH area = π(20,015² − 10,008²) ≈ 9.45 × 10⁸ km² — SH is 3× NH area.
match_precision: N/A — structural dependency, not a constant match
why_it_matters: Three layers of globe dependency: (1) CERES instruments orbit on globe-model trajectories; their orbit predictions use Keplerian mechanics. (2) The conversion from observed radiances to W/m² fluxes uses Angular Distribution Models (ADMs) built for a spherical Earth — on a flat disc, the same satellite observations would yield different flux values because the viewing geometry differs. (3) 'Hemispheric average' means integrating over equal-area hemispheres — on the dome's disc, the SH has 3× the area of the NH, so a per-unit-area comparison would give fundamentally different numbers. The dome adopts a number whose very meaning depends on globe geometry.

finding_1_ceres_framework.description: CERES (Clouds and Earth's Radiant Energy System) produces the hemispheric energy budget data from which +0.34 W/m²/decade is derived. The processing pipeline (CERES-EBAF, Edition 4.2) converts broadband radiances measured from sun-synchronous orbit into TOA fluxes using scene-type-dependent Angular Distribution Models. These ADMs are constructed from multi-angle observations assuming the Earth is an oblate spheroid (WGS84). On a flat disc under a local sun, the same satellite detector readings would map to completely different flux values because (a) the satellite's position relative to the surface below is modeled differently, (b) the sun-surface-satellite geometry changes, and (c) the integration over 'hemispheric area' involves different geometries.
finding_1_ceres_framework.significance: The W/m² number is not a raw observation — it is a highly processed quantity whose processing chain assumes spherical Earth geometry at every step. The dome cannot claim this number as evidence without inheriting the globe geometry that produced it.

finding_2_hemisphere_area_ratio.description: On the globe, NH and SH each have area 2πR² ≈ 2.55 × 10⁸ km². On the dome's AE-projection disc, the equator maps to r = πR/2 ≈ 10,008 km. The NH (inner circle) has area π(10,008)² ≈ 3.15 × 10⁸ km². The SH (annular ring from 10,008 to 20,015 km) has area π(20,015² − 10,008²) ≈ 9.45 × 10⁸ km² — exactly 3× the NH area. This 1:3 ratio is an intrinsic consequence of the AE projection stretching southern regions. If the dome computed a hemispheric energy budget on its own geometry, the SH average would be diluted by 3× the area, giving a completely different NH-SH asymmetry value.
finding_2_hemisphere_area_ratio.significance: The dome can only claim the observed hemispheric asymmetry matches its expectations if it uses the globe's equal-area hemispheres for the comparison. Computing the budget on the dome's own disc geometry would give a different number. This is another case where the dome's 'prediction' only works when you use globe math to evaluate it.

finding_3_no_dome_radiation_model.description: On the dome's disc, the sun orbits at 5,733 km altitude in circles of varying radius (smaller for NH summer, larger for NH winter). Solar flux at any point depends on inverse-square distance to the local sun plus viewing angle. Integrating this over the disc to get a hemispheric energy budget is a tractable calculation the dome has never performed. If they did: the inner disc (NH) receives more concentrated illumination (closer to sun's orbit) while the outer annulus (SH) receives more dispersed illumination. But quantifying this requires dome parameters, and no such calculation appears anywhere on the dome site or in its repository code.
finding_3_no_dome_radiation_model.significance: The dome has the geometry to make a quantitative prediction about hemispheric energy asymmetry but hasn't done so. Instead, it adopts a globe-framework measurement. If the dome DID compute its own radiation budget, the answer would almost certainly not be +0.34 W/m²/decade because (a) the SH area is 3× larger, diluting the per-unit-area difference, (b) the sun's orbital radius variation doesn't map to the specific aerosol/ice/cloud changes driving the observed trend, and (c) the dome geometry is static while the observed quantity is a time-dependent trend.

finding_4_disc_radius_equals_pi_R.description: The dome's disc radius a = 20,015 km = π × 6,371 km = π × R_Earth. The equator at r = a/2 = 10,008 km ≈ πR/2. This means every 'hemispheric' quantity on the dome — including the NH/SH energy budget — has its geographic boundaries set by π × R_Earth, the globe's half-circumference. The dome inherits globe geography through its fundamental scale parameter.
finding_4_disc_radius_equals_pi_R.significance: Already noted in many previous fingerprints (WIN-056, WIN-062, etc.), but relevant here because the hemisphere boundary itself is a globe-derived parameter.

code_search.found_in: No direct reference to WIN-066 or hemispheric energy in dome raw text. WIN-066 is a V51.0 addition not present in the V50.6 baseline snapshot.
code_search.notes: GitHub CLI unavailable for direct repo code search. Based on extracted raw-text and curmudgeon's Cycle 1 review: no monitoring code, no calculation script, no radiation budget formula exists for this WIN. The dome's repository contains zero climate energy budget computation. The dome's live power dashboard (08-live-power.txt) shows 39 domains but none for hemispheric energy or CERES data.

suggested_text: The dome's claim adopts a measurement whose every component assumes globe geometry. The +0.34 W/m²/decade figure derives from CERES satellite data, where radiances are converted to fluxes using Angular Distribution Models calibrated for a spherical Earth, and 'hemispheric average' means integrating over equal-area hemispheres. On the dome's disc, the southern 'hemisphere' has 3× the area of the northern (a geometric consequence of the AE projection), meaning any per-unit-area energy budget computed on dome geometry would give fundamentally different values. The dome has the parameters to calculate its own hemispheric energy budget — local sun orbit, disc geometry, firmament height — but no such calculation exists. The claim is a globe-framework measurement relabeled as dome confirmation.
